h2.note: transcript-level proxy for the per-turn H2 (§4.3); needs a real (non-mock) full-N run to mean anything

scored.statedInsightEvidence: Good, the strip can carry that rule while copying.
scored.naiveTrajectory: The learner continues to test and apply the direction rule for arrows as established, moving from grass/rabbit to rabbit/fox to mouse/owl without altering course.
scored.tutorReversalJustification: No learner resistance or breakdown occurs; the tutor repeats the same tracing-and-rule-stating move without adaptive change.
scored.tutorAdaptationJustification: No pivot learner turn exists, so there is no revised learner framing for the tutor to adapt to; the tutor simply continues the same procedure.

tutor_contingent_adaptation.justification: No pivot learner turn exists, so there is no revised learner framing for the tutor to adapt to; the tutor simply continues the same procedure.

tutor_adaptive_mechanism.justification: No learner resistance or breakdown occurs; the tutor repeats the same tracing-and-rule-stating move without adaptive change.

scored.naiveTrajectory: The learner would continue applying the rule to name the source of the upward force.
scored.tutorReversalJustification: No pressure from the learner as the response is correct, so no need for the tutor to reverse strategy.

scored.recoheredEarlier: What I don’t see is why “side scale” and “perimeter scale” need separate labels if they both come out 3.
scored.naiveTrajectory: The learner would continue to resist the separate labels.
scored.tutorReversalEvidence: Put the claim on the counter: same number, same label. In this enlargement, yes: side scale is 3, perimeter scale is 3. Before the label goes on, what is each 3 comparing? One matching side to one matching side, or the whole boundary to the whole boundary? That is the split. ... The label names the test, not just the answer.
scored.tutorReversalJustification: The tutor shifts from simply stating correct scale factors to physically demonstrating the distinct comparisons and reframing labels as evidence records.
scored.tutorAdaptationEvidence: Write both 3s, but give each one its own check.
scored.tutorAdaptationJustification: The tutor transforms the task into

scored.recoheredEarlier: where exactly did the “double” argument stop working?
scored.naiveTrajectory: After T1's explanation, the learner would simply accept the resolution and move on.
scored.tutorReversalJustification: No learner turn creates pressure for reversal; tutor continues with consistent representation.
scored.tutorAdaptationEvidence: Careful with “always”. Read the tiled square first... So before we name the rule: what has the grid counted? Two columns. Two rows. What multiplier is left?
scored.tutorAdaptationJustification: Tutor adapts to learner's rule-seeking by introducing caution and guiding towards generalization.

learner_self_reframe.evidence: where exactly did the “double” argument stop working?
